item.title: Issue: Migrations: Drop generated column should come before dropping the columns it's generated from - @benstevens48
item.description: Using EF core 5, I removed three columns from my model, with the third column being of type NpgsqlTsVector and computed from the other two via HasGeneratedTsVectorColumn. When I generated the SQL using migrations, it tried to drop the generated column after the other two. However, PostgreSQL doesn't seem to like this - I guess the generated column is already dropped when the columns it's generated from are dropped. It would be good if the order was changed so the generated column was dropped first.

item.title: Issue: Query/Test: extend temporal query test infra to allow rewriting of queries that are not using AssertQuery - @maumar
item.description: Currently we can rewrite tests using AssertQuery into temporal query. The current infra matches ISetSource.Set method, we could extend that to also work on regular queries.

item.title: Issue: Two Letter Acronyms naming convention vs DbContext - @PingPongSet
item.description:
According to the convention below, DbContext should be called DBContext instead. Or is it based on a newer naming convention?

Do capitalize both characters of two-character acronyms, except the first word of a camel-cased identifier.

https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/dotnet/netframework-4.0/ms229043(v=vs.100)#capitalization-rules-for-acronyms

EF Core version: All

item.title: Issue: Migrations: Add support for non-derived contexts - @AndriySvyryd
item.description:
var modelBuilder = SqliteConventionSetBuilder.CreateModelBuilder();
modelBuilder.Entity<Blog>();
var model = modelBuilder.FinalizeModel();
builder.Services.AddDbContext<DbContext>(c => c.UseSqlite("Data Source=MyDb.db").UseModel(model));

No DbContext was found in assembly 'WebApp'. Ensure that you're using the correct assembly and that the type is neither abstract nor generic.

item.title: Issue: AsSplitQuery undermines OrderBy clause of an Include with a ThenInclude - @pjaffe
item.description:
An .OrderBy(...) clause in a filtered .Include(...) does not take effect as expected. I empirically isolated it to the combination of both a .ThenInclude(...) and the .AsSplitQuery() directive. If I eliminate one or the other of those, the .OrderBy(...) starts working as expected.

I would assume both should interplay nicely based on Filtered Includes being documented as supporting OrderBy clauses, and the noted limitations didn't seem to indicate any incompatibilities with the use of Split Queries.

Here is my code block:

await _dbOps.Db.CustomLists
  .Include(x => x.Items.OrderBy(y => y.DisplaySequence).ThenBy(y => y.Name))
  .ThenInclude(x => x.Restrictions)
  // BUG: The combo of the ThenInclude above + the SplitQuery below undermines the sorting of the Items collection
  // Note that a Take and/or Where would still work fine with the AsSplitQuery in place.
  // Commenting out the AsSplitQuery() causes the OrderBy to take effect as expected
  .AsSplitQuery()
  .ToListAsync()

I'm hopeful this is enough to reproduce the problem in whatever test harnesses are in the EF Core project. But if capturing verbose logging from my running system would help, I could grab that.

Again, in brief, the .OrderBy(...) clauses above take effect if I either:

  • comment out the .AsSplitQuery()
  • or comment out the .ThenInclude(...)

But I would expect the full clause above to work as written. This seems to be specific to OrderBy, since a Take or Where is applied as expected in all situations.

EF Core version: 5.0.7 Database provider: Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL Target framework: (e.g. .NET 5.0) Operating system: running in a mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:5.0.103-focal docker container IDE: VS Code 1.58.0 using Remote Containers

item.title: Issue: EF Core Cosmos: Owned Embedded Docs id property - @jcookpob
item.description:
Have an existing cosmosdb database with over a billion docs so changing schema is a little difficult.

One of our embedded owned properties is Tags which is an ICollection Tags {get; set;} = new HashSet(); we have another property that is similar and works great but the only difference is that Tag has 2 properties id and name. I can't find a way to get id to save to cosmos using EF Core 5 and i am assuming because EF is using this property name under the hood for tracking purposes. Instead of changing the property field to something other than id which appears to work fine and try to update over a billion documents is there a way to configure EF Core 5 to use a different field for its indexing so we can continue using the id property?

No matter what i do i can't get ef to save the id value I set in code but it does save the name property to cosmos. When i read the record back through EF Core it has a generated value in the id property.

currently using .NET 5 EntityFrameworkCore v5.0.7 EntityFrameworkCore.Cosmos v5.0.7 and Microsoft.Azure.Cosmos v3.20.1

item.title: Issue: EF generates two nonsensical JOINs when mapping a HasOne().WithOne() relationship with table splitting - @InspiringCode
item.description:
Given a very simple model with a one-to-one relationship using table splitting:

public class Employee {
    public Guid Id { get; set; } = Guid.NewGuid();

    public string Name { get; set; }

    public EmployeeData Data { get; set; }
}

public class EmployeeData {
    public string Address { get; set; }
}

With the mapping

b.Entity<Employee>(e => {
    e.ToTable("Employee");
    e.HasOne(x => x.Data).WithOne().HasForeignKey<EmployeeData>("Id");
});

b.Entity<EmployeeData>(e => {
    e.ToTable("Employee");
    e.HasKey("Id");
    e.Property<Guid>("Id");
});

When simply querying for all Employees:

db.Set<Employee>().Include(x => x.Data).ToArray()

Then the query should have no JOINs since all data is in the Employee table, but the actual query has TWO JOINs!

SELECT [e].[Id], [e].[Name], [t].[Id], [t].[Address]
FROM [Employee] AS [e]
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT [e0].[Id], [e0].[Address]
    FROM [Employee] AS [e0]
    INNER JOIN [Employee] AS [e1] ON [e0].[Id] = [e1].[Id]
) AS [t] ON [e].[Id] = [t].[Id]

Even when I directly query for EmployeeData with db.Set<EmployeeData>().ToArray(), a strange INNER JOIN is generated:

SELECT [e].[Id], [e].[Address]
FROM [Employee] AS [e]
INNER JOIN [Employee] AS [e0] ON [e].[Id] = [e0].[Id]

This seems like a bug to me, or am I using Entity Framework Core in a very wrong way here? Is there any way I can change my mapping so that the queries make more sense?

I am using Entity Framework Core 5.0.7.

item.title: Issue: No DbContext was found in assembly 'CoreDatabaseStandard'. - @kbrow20
item.description:
I am getting the following error:

No DbContext was found in assembly 'CoreDatabaseStandard'. Ensure that you're using the correct assembly and that the type is neither abstract nor generic.

I had to downgrade my EF project thanks to PhotonServer not supporting Core .Net 1 to 3 and .Net 5. So I had to make my new EF project 2.0 Net Standard Library to have it work with .Net Framework. It was working previously when I had all my projects at .Net5. So I have a PhotonServer application that is running at .Net 4.7.2 and WebAPI that is running at .Net5.

So I am having a hard time running Add-Migration Initialize -Project CoreDatabaseStandard

Plus in my WebAPI it seems that it isn't getting the users out of the database that was created from my previous database project that was using .Net5.

        [UseDbContext(typeof(AppDbContext))]
         public async Task<AuthenticatedToken> GetAuthenticationToken(AuthenticationTokenInput authenticationTokenInput, [ScopedService] AppDbContext context, [Service] UserManager<AccountUser> userManager, [Service] ITopicEventSender eventSender, CancellationToken cancellationToken)
         {
              AccountUser user = await userManager.FindByNameAsync(authenticationTokenInput.UserName); <= Errors out right here
              bool confirmed = await userManager.CheckPasswordAsync(user, authenticationTokenInput.Password);
         }

using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;

using System;

using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

namespace CoreDatabaseStandard.EntityFramework
{
      public class AppDbContext : IdentityDbContext<AccountUser, IdentityRole<int>, int>
      {
      }
}

I also can't seem to find a good way with my AppDbContext to create a Initializer so I can populate data into my tables. I find plenty on how to do it with EntityFramework that isn't related to IdentityDbContext. I just want to do this:

public DbSet<Galaxy> Galaxies { get; set; } <= Insert MilkyWay
public DbSet<Planet> Planets { get; set; } <= Insert Earth, Mars, and etc

item.title: Issue: Reload(Async) does not fire PropertyChang(ing|ed) with Castle.Core-proxies - @AliveDevil
item.description:
File a bug

According to #19340 Reload and ReloadAsync should fire PropertyChanged and PropertyChanging for entities implementing INotifyPropertyChanged and INotifyPropertyChanging respectively.

Though with EF Core 5.0 and the new Castle.Core-proxies implementation this does not happen anymore. With ReactiveUI this is especially cumbersome as I have to manually update states here now.

I created a sample reproduction repository: https://github.com/AliveDevil/efcore-reload (additionally attached as efcore-reload-main.zip).

Following is happening:

  • Print a.B?.Id (which is empty)
  • Subscribe to PropertyChanging and PropertyChanged-events
    • Those should print PropertyName to Console for Changing and Changed.
  • run LoadAsync on B-DbSet
    • Reprint a.B?.Id and observe that the Id has changed
  • run Entry(a).ReloadAsync() - atleast here PropertyChanged or PropertyChanging should be fired
  • reprint a.B?.Id and observe nothing has changed (between this and after LoadAsync)

Include verbose output

Running the app produces:

# Scenario 1 #
info: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Infrastructure[10403]
      Entity Framework Core 5.0.7 initialized 'AppDbContext' using provider 'Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Sqlite' with options: using change tracking proxies 
warn: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Query[10103]
      The query uses the 'First'/'FirstOrDefault' operator without 'OrderBy' and filter operators. This may lead to unpredictable results.
info: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command[20101]
      Executed DbCommand (0ms) [Parameters=[], CommandType='Text', CommandTimeout='30']
      SELECT "a"."Id", "a"."BId1"
      FROM "A" AS "a"
      LIMIT 1

info: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command[20101]
      Executed DbCommand (0ms) [Parameters=[], CommandType='Text', CommandTimeout='30']
      SELECT "b"."Id"
      FROM "B" AS "b"
1
Before Reload
info: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command[20101]
      Executed DbCommand (3ms) [Parameters=[@__p_0='?'], CommandType='Text', CommandTimeout='30']
      SELECT "a"."Id", "a"."BId1"
      FROM "A" AS "a"
      WHERE "a"."Id" = @__p_0
      LIMIT 1
After Reload Reload
1
# Scenario 2 #
info: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Infrastructure[10403]
      Entity Framework Core 5.0.7 initialized 'AppDbContext' using provider 'Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Sqlite' with options: using change tracking proxies 
info: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command[20101]
      Executed DbCommand (1ms) [Parameters=[], CommandType='Text', CommandTimeout='30']
      SELECT "a"."Id", "a"."BId1"
      FROM "A" AS "a"
      LIMIT 1

info: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Infrastructure[10403]
      Entity Framework Core 5.0.7 initialized 'AppDbContext' using provider 'Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Sqlite' with options: using change tracking proxies 
info: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command[20101]
      Executed DbCommand (0ms) [Parameters=[], CommandType='Text', CommandTimeout='30']
      SELECT "b"."Id"
      FROM "B" AS "b"

info: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command[20101]
      Executed DbCommand (1ms) [Parameters=[@__p_0='?'], CommandType='Text', CommandTimeout='30']
      SELECT "a"."Id", "a"."BId1"
      FROM "A" AS "a"
      WHERE "a"."Id" = @__p_0
      LIMIT 1
1

Observe that there is no entry starting with Changing or Changed.

I expect the proxies to implement basic change tracking themselves, and fire PropertyChanged and PropertyChanging accordingly.

Include provider and version information

EF Core version: 5.0.7 Database provider: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Sqlite Target framework: .NET 5.0 Operating system: OS agnostic (observed on .NET Console macOS and .NET WPF Windows) IDE: IDE agnostic (observed on Visual Studio for Mac 8.10.5 and Visual Studio 2019 16.10.3)

- independentCommandSet.Sort(_modificationCommandComparer);
-
- var batch = _modificationCommandBatchFactory.Create();
- foreach (var modificationCommand in independentCommandSet)

+
+ var batch = _modificationCommandBatchFactory.Create();
+ foreach (var modificationCommand in independentCommandSetSorted)

item.title: Issue: InvalidCastException when querying with date parameter - @natehitze
item.description:
Include your code

Query:

LocalDate startSearchDate = ...;
LocalDate endSearchDate = ...;
var configurationEvents = await Context.Events
				.AsNoTracking()
				.Where(e => e.IsPublic)
				.Where(e => e.StartDate > startSearchDate && e.EndDate < endSearchDate)
				.ToListAsync();

Model:

public class Event 
{
    public LocalDate? StartDate { get; set; }
    public LocalDate? EndDate { get; set; }
    public bool IsPublic { get; set; }
}

We use a value converter to translate NodaTime types to DateTime:

// Based on code from https://andrewlock.net/strongly-typed-ids-in-ef-core-using-strongly-typed-entity-ids-to-avoid-primitive-obsession-part-4
public class MyValueConverterSelector : ValueConverterSelector
    {
        // The dictionary in the base type is private, so we need our own one here.
        private readonly ConcurrentDictionary<(Type ModelClrType, Type ProviderClrType), ValueConverterInfo> _converters
            = new ConcurrentDictionary<(Type ModelClrType, Type ProviderClrType), ValueConverterInfo>();

        public MyValueConverterSelector([NotNull] ValueConverterSelectorDependencies dependencies) : base(dependencies) { }

        public override IEnumerable<ValueConverterInfo> Select(Type modelClrType, Type providerClrType = null)
        {
            var baseConverters = base.Select(modelClrType, providerClrType);
            foreach (var converter in baseConverters)
            {
                yield return converter;
            }

            // Extract the "real" type T from Nullable<T> if required
            var underlyingModelType = UnwrapNullableType(modelClrType);
            var underlyingProviderType = UnwrapNullableType(providerClrType);

            if (underlyingModelType == typeof(Instant))
            {
                if (underlyingProviderType == null || underlyingProviderType == typeof(DateTime))
                {
                    yield return _converters.GetOrAdd((underlyingModelType, typeof(DateTime)), k => NodaTimeInstantValueConverter.DefaultInfo);
                }
            }

            if (underlyingModelType == typeof(LocalDate))
            {
                if (underlyingProviderType == null || underlyingProviderType == typeof(DateTime))
                {
                    yield return _converters.GetOrAdd((underlyingModelType, typeof(DateTime)), k => NodaTimeLocalDateValueConverter.DefaultInfo);
                }
            }

            if (underlyingModelType == typeof(LocalTime))
            {
                if (underlyingProviderType == null || underlyingProviderType == typeof(DateTime))
                {
                    yield return _converters.GetOrAdd((underlyingModelType, typeof(DateTime)), k => NodaTimeLocalTimeValueConverter.DefaultInfo);
                }
            }

            if (underlyingModelType == typeof(DateTimeZone))
            {
                if (underlyingProviderType == null || underlyingProviderType == typeof(string))
                {
                    yield return _converters.GetOrAdd((underlyingModelType, typeof(string)), k => NodaTimeDateTimeZoneValueConverter.DefaultInfo);
                }
            }
        }

        private static Type UnwrapNullableType(Type type)
        {
            if (type is null) { return null; }

            return Nullable.GetUnderlyingType(type) ?? type;
        }
    }

Include stack traces

System.InvalidCastException: Unable to cast object of type 'System.Data.SqlClient.SqlParameter' to type 'Microsoft.Data.SqlClient.SqlParameter'.

item.title: Issue: Internal exceptions when using .AsSplitQuery - @natehitze
item.description:
Include your code

I'm trying to reproduce locally but this issue is currently only happening in our production environment. It is rare but comes up every few minutes on the couple endpoints that I'm using .AsSplitQuery() with.

The query looks like this:

var events = await _dbContext.Events
                    .AsNoTracking()
                    .Where(...query...)
                    .AsSplitQuery()
                    .ProjectTo<DtoEvent>(MapperConfiguration)
                    .ToListAsync(cancellationToken);

My context is registered like this:

services.AddDbContextPool<MyDbContext>(opt =>
    {
        opt.ReplaceService<IValueConverterSelector, MyValueConverterSelector>();
        opt.UseSqlServer(Configuration["MyDBConnectionString"], sqlOpt => 
        { 
            sqlOpt.UseNetTopologySuite();
            sqlOpt.EnableRetryOnFailure();

        opt.ConfigureWarnings(warnings =>

item.title: Issue: EF Core Issues with Owned Types in multiple Many-To-Many Relationships between two entities - @bbartels
item.description:
File a bug

I was trying to create two many-to-many relationships between the same types. This does not cause any issues until a related entity has two or more owned types (it works fine with a single owned type).

I've searched previously filed issue and could not find , however there are so many and the exception message is very generic that I might have missed it.

Include your code

Below is the offending DBContext, I've attached a full reproduction here: https://gist.github.com/bbartels/685bfd47a20f1e7db85f89af64b3422f

  builder.Entity<Item>(e =>
  {
      e.HasKey(x => x.Guid);
  });

  builder.Entity<Lab>(e =>
  {
      e.HasKey(e => e.Guid);
      e.Property(x => x.OtherId).IsRequired();
      e.OwnsOne(x => x.Value1);
      e.OwnsOne(x => x.Value2);
  });

  builder.Entity<Other>(e =>
  {
      e.HasKey(x => x.Guid);
      e.HasMany(x => x.Items).WithMany("Groups1");
      e.HasMany(x => x.OtherItems).WithMany("Groups2");
      e.HasMany(x => x.Labs).WithOne().IsRequired().HasForeignKey(x => x.OtherId);
  });

Include stack traces

  Message: 
    System.ArgumentException : An item with the same key has already been added. Key: MinRepro.ValueEntity

  Stack Trace: 
    Dictionary`2.TryInsert(TKey key, TValue value, InsertionBehavior behavior)
    Enumerable.ToDictionary[TSource,TKey,TElement](IEnumerable`1 source, Func`2 keySelector, Func`2 elementSelector, IEqualityComparer`1 comparer)
    Enumerable.ToDictionary[TSource,TKey,TElement](IEnumerable`1 source, Func`2 keySelector, Func`2 elementSelector)
    ManyToManyJoinEntityTypeConvention.CreateJoinEntityType(IConventionSkipNavigationBuilder skipNavigationBuilder)
    ManyToManyJoinEntityTypeConvention.ProcessSkipNavigationInverseChanged(IConventionSkipNavigationBuilder skipNavigationBuilder, IConventionSkipNavigation inverse, IConventionSkipNavigation oldInverse, IConventionContext`1 context)
    ImmediateConventionScope.OnSkipNavigationInverseChanged(IConventionSkipNavigationBuilder navigationBuilder, IConventionSkipNavigation inverse, IConventionSkipNavigation oldInverse)
    OnSkipNavigationInverseChangedNode.Run(ConventionDispatcher dispatcher)
    DelayedConventionScope.Run(ConventionDispatcher dispatcher)
    ConventionBatch.Run()
    ConventionBatch.Dispose()
    <28 more frames...>
    DbContext.get_DbContextDependencies()
    DbContext.get_InternalServiceProvider()
    IServiceProvider>.get_Instance()
    InfrastructureExtensions.GetService[TService](IInfrastructure`1 accessor)
    AccessorExtensions.GetService[TService](IInfrastructure`1 accessor)
    DatabaseFacade.get_Dependencies()
    DatabaseFacade.CreateExecutionStrategy()
    RelationalDatabaseFacadeExtensions.OpenConnection(DatabaseFacade databaseFacade)
    UnitTest1.FailingTest() line 90

Include verbose output

Verbose output seems to have no important information, I can include it if desired.

Include provider and version information

EF Core version: 5.0.7 Database provider: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Sqlite Target framework: .NET 5.0 Operating system: Windows IDE: Visual Studio 2022 Preview 1

item.title: Issue: Support file-scoped namespace declarations in generated .cs files - @DamianEdwards
item.description:
C# 10 introduces support for file-scoped namespace declarations and the .NET 6 templates are being updated to use this new syntax .

It would be great if the .cs files generated into the project by the EF tooling (dotnet ef) supported emitting namespaces using this new syntax. It could either:

  • simply default to using the new syntax when the project targets .NET 6 (unless the project explicitly targets a lower version of C#)
  • perhaps in addition sniff the content of .cs files in the project to determine which namespace declaration syntax the project is using and match that (e.g. match the first namespace declaration found in a .cs file with a depth-first search from project root)
  • default to using the new syntax for .NET 6 targeted projects and support an option to opt-out of this behavior

item.title: Issue: System.NotSupportedException: Serialization and deserialization of 'System.Type' - @uzairali001
item.description:
Issue

When I'm trying to get data from db using linq with this code

var _entity = context.Set<TEntity>();
...
return await _entity
.Where(u => u.UserEmail == emailAddress)
.Where(u => u.IsActive == true)
.Where(u => u.IsDeleted == false)
.FirstOrDefaultAsync(cancellationToken);

It throws this exception

{"Cannot access a disposed context instance. A common cause of this error is disposing a context instance that was resolved from dependency injection and then later trying to use the same context instance elsewhere in your application. This may occur if you are calling 'Dispose' on the context instance, or wrapping it in a using statement. If you are using dependency injection, you should let the dependency injection container take care of disposing context instances.\r\nObject name: 'MyAuthDbContext'."}

item.title: Issue: Can't find single property and navigate related data without IQueryable all properties - @BretMishler
item.description:
I have relational data properties on my objects that I would like to access:

    public class Person
    {
       public ICollection<Address> Addresses { get; private set; } = new Collection<Address>();
       ...
    }

    public class Address
    {
       ...
       public int PersonId { get; set; }
       public Person Person { get; set; }
       ...
    }

The only way I have found to traverse, or even exercise, this navigation property without lazy loading (and pulling in proxies, etc) is to eager load it:

    var entityList = await _context.Persons.Where(p => p.Id == request.PersonId)
        .Include(p => p.Addresses)
        .ToListAsync();

which is inconvenient because now I always have to do the following in order to cleanly access the class (and its properties):

var person = entityList.FirstOrDefault();

Every example in the Microsoft docs:

    var blogs = context.Blogs
        .Include(blog => blog.Posts)
        .ToList();

they grab every blog entity, instead of just a single one, before loading in related data.

I can't Explicitly load because that relies on the public virtual EntityEntry<TEntity> Entry<TEntity>(TEntity entity) which I can't accesses via my injected DbContext interface.

Is there a cleaner way to do this without the IQuerable where expression? I simply want to find my single entity and navigate its properties.

EF Core version: 5.0.6 Database provider: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.SqlServer Target framework: .NET 5.0 Operating system: Win 10 IDE: Visual Studio 2019 16.3

item.title: Issue: Including related entities causes performance degradation - @yellfage
item.description:
The next LINQ translates into SQL, performance of which degrades depending on the number of Message entities. Below you can find a test project where you can test this behavior.

EF Core version: 5.0.7 Database providers: Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL (5.0.7), Pomelo.EntityFrameworkCore.MySql (5.0.1) Target framework: .NET 5.0 Operating system: Windows 10 IDE: Visual Studio 2019 16.9

Test project: EFCore.Issue.zip

builder
    .Entity<Message>()
    .HasIndex(message => new { message.ChatId, message.CreatedAt })
    .HasSortOrder(SortOrder.Ascending, SortOrder.Descending);

IList<Chat> = context.Chats
    .Include(chat => chat.Messages
        .OrderByDescending(message => message.CreatedAt)
        .Take(1))
    .Where(chat => chat.Id == 1)
    .ToList();

Generated SQL by EF Core:

SELECT c."Id",
       t0."Id",
       t0."ChatId",
       t0."CreatedAt"
FROM "Chats" AS c
LEFT JOIN
  (SELECT t."Id",
          t."ChatId",
          t."CreatedAt"
   FROM
     (SELECT m."Id",
             m."ChatId",
             m."CreatedAt",
             ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY m."ChatId"
                               ORDER BY m."CreatedAt" DESC) AS ROW
      FROM "Messages" AS m) AS t
   WHERE t.row <= 1 ) AS t0 ON c."Id" = t0."ChatId"
WHERE c."Id" = 1
ORDER BY c."Id",
         t0."ChatId",
         t0."CreatedAt" DESC,
         t0."Id"

100,000 messages:

"Sort  (cost=11682.09..11765.42 rows=33333 width=20) (actual time=52.309..52.310 rows=1 loops=1)"
"  Sort Key: t.""ChatId"", t.""CreatedAt"" DESC, t.""Id"""
"  Sort Method: quicksort  Memory: 25kB"
"  ->  Nested Loop Left Join  (cost=0.57..9178.00 rows=33333 width=20) (actual time=0.036..52.304 rows=1 loops=1)"
"        Join Filter: (c.""Id"" = t.""ChatId"")"
"        ->  Index Only Scan using ""PK_Chats"" on ""Chats"" c  (cost=0.15..8.17 rows=1 width=4) (actual time=0.014..0.015 rows=1 loops=1)"
"              Index Cond: (""Id"" = 1)"
"              Heap Fetches: 1"
"        ->  Subquery Scan on t  (cost=0.42..8753.17 rows=33333 width=16) (actual time=0.021..52.286 rows=1 loops=1)"
"              Filter: (t.""row"" <= 1)"
"              Rows Removed by Filter: 99999"
"              ->  WindowAgg  (cost=0.42..7503.17 rows=100000 width=24) (actual time=0.020..48.532 rows=100000 loops=1)"
"                    ->  Index Scan using ""IX_Messages_ChatId_CreatedAt"" on ""Messages"" m  (cost=0.42..5753.17 rows=100000 width=16) (actual time=0.013..16.468 rows=100000 loops=1)"
"                          Index Cond: (""ChatId"" = 1)"
"Planning Time: 0.146 ms"
"Execution Time: 52.336 ms"

1,000,000 messages:

"Sort  (cost=135280.22..136113.55 rows=333333 width=20) (actual time=503.943..503.944 rows=1 loops=1)"
"  Sort Key: t.""ChatId"", t.""CreatedAt"" DESC, t.""Id"""
"  Sort Method: quicksort  Memory: 25kB"
"  ->  Nested Loop Left Join  (cost=0.58..97863.57 rows=333333 width=20) (actual time=0.026..503.940 rows=1 loops=1)"
"        Join Filter: (c.""Id"" = t.""ChatId"")"
"        ->  Index Only Scan using ""PK_Chats"" on ""Chats"" c  (cost=0.15..8.17 rows=1 width=4) (actual time=0.010..0.011 rows=1 loops=1)"
"              Index Cond: (""Id"" = 1)"
"              Heap Fetches: 1"
"        ->  Subquery Scan on t  (cost=0.42..93688.74 rows=333333 width=16) (actual time=0.015..503.926 rows=1 loops=1)"
"              Filter: (t.""row"" <= 1)"
"              Rows Removed by Filter: 999999"
"              ->  WindowAgg  (cost=0.42..81188.74 rows=1000000 width=24) (actual time=0.014..465.909 rows=1000000 loops=1)"
"                    ->  Index Scan using ""IX_Messages_ChatId_CreatedAt"" on ""Messages"" m  (cost=0.42..63688.74 rows=1000000 width=16) (actual time=0.009..154.595 rows=1000000 loops=1)"
"                          Index Cond: (""ChatId"" = 1)"
"Planning Time: 0.128 ms"
"Execution Time: 503.968 ms"

A much more efficient SQL might look like this:

SELECT c."Id",
       t0."Id",
       t0."ChatId",
       t0."CreatedAt"
FROM "Chats" AS c
LEFT JOIN LATERAL -- Or CROSS JOIN LATERAL
  (SELECT m."Id",
          m."ChatId",
          m."CreatedAt"
   FROM "Messages" AS m
   WHERE m."ChatId" = c."Id"
   ORDER BY m."CreatedAt" DESC
   LIMIT 1) AS t0 ON c."Id" = t0."ChatId"
WHERE c."Id" = 1
ORDER BY c."Id",
         t0."ChatId",
         t0."CreatedAt" DESC,
         t0."Id"

100,000 messages:

"Sort  (cost=8.68..8.69 rows=1 width=20) (actual time=0.025..0.025 rows=1 loops=1)"
"  Sort Key: t0.""ChatId"", t0.""CreatedAt"" DESC, t0.""Id"""
"  Sort Method: quicksort  Memory: 25kB"
"  ->  Nested Loop Left Join  (cost=0.57..8.67 rows=1 width=20) (actual time=0.019..0.020 rows=1 loops=1)"
"        ->  Index Only Scan using ""PK_Chats"" on ""Chats"" c  (cost=0.15..8.17 rows=1 width=4) (actual time=0.009..0.010 rows=1 loops=1)"
"              Index Cond: (""Id"" = 1)"
"              Heap Fetches: 1"
"        ->  Subquery Scan on t0  (cost=0.42..0.49 rows=1 width=16) (actual time=0.009..0.009 rows=1 loops=1)"
"              Filter: ((t0.""ChatId"" = 1) AND (c.""Id"" = t0.""ChatId""))"
"              ->  Limit  (cost=0.42..0.48 rows=1 width=16) (actual time=0.008..0.008 rows=1 loops=1)"
"                    ->  Index Scan using ""IX_Messages_ChatId_CreatedAt"" on ""Messages"" m  (cost=0.42..5753.17 rows=100000 width=16) (actual time=0.007..0.007 rows=1 loops=1)"
"                          Index Cond: (""ChatId"" = c.""Id"")"
"Planning Time: 0.121 ms"
"Execution Time: 0.044 ms"

1,000,000 messages:

"Sort  (cost=8.70..8.70 rows=1 width=20) (actual time=0.038..0.039 rows=1 loops=1)"
"  Sort Key: t0.""ChatId"", t0.""CreatedAt"" DESC, t0.""Id"""
"  Sort Method: quicksort  Memory: 25kB"
"  ->  Nested Loop Left Join  (cost=0.58..8.69 rows=1 width=20) (actual time=0.029..0.030 rows=1 loops=1)"
"        ->  Index Only Scan using ""PK_Chats"" on ""Chats"" c  (cost=0.15..8.17 rows=1 width=4) (actual time=0.013..0.013 rows=1 loops=1)"
"              Index Cond: (""Id"" = 1)"
"              Heap Fetches: 1"
"        ->  Subquery Scan on t0  (cost=0.42..0.50 rows=1 width=16) (actual time=0.015..0.015 rows=1 loops=1)"
"              Filter: ((t0.""ChatId"" = 1) AND (c.""Id"" = t0.""ChatId""))"
"              ->  Limit  (cost=0.42..0.49 rows=1 width=16) (actual time=0.014..0.014 rows=1 loops=1)"
"                    ->  Index Scan using ""IX_Messages_ChatId_CreatedAt"" on ""Messages"" m  (cost=0.42..63688.74 rows=1000000 width=16) (actual time=0.013..0.013 rows=1 loops=1)"
"                          Index Cond: (""ChatId"" = c.""Id"")"
"Planning Time: 0.122 ms"
"Execution Time: 0.057 ms"

item.title: Issue: Cannot assign `null` to optional relationship if a shadow foreign key is used. - @denschub
item.description:
Assuming the following models:

public class Banana {
  public int Id { get; set; }
  public Consumer? Consumer { get; set; }
}

public class Consumer {
  public int Id { get; set; }
}

Assigning a Consumer to a Banana works fine with:

myBanana.Consumer = dbContext.Consumers.First(row => row.Id == 1);
dbContext.Update(myBanana);
dbContext.SaveChanges();

Even though there is no explicit ConsumerId property as foreign key, the documentation explicitly states

While it is recommended to have a foreign key property defined in the dependent entity class, it is not required. If no foreign key property is found, a shadow foreign key property will be introduced

and that works just fine, as the migration built a ConsumerId database column and is updating that.

However, it's not possible to assign null to that relation ever again, as trying to set it via

myBanana.Consumer = null;
dbContext.Update(myBanana);
dbContext.SaveChanges();

doesn't actually update the ConsumerId database column. You can still manually set the shadow foreign key property to null using

dbContext.Entry(myBanana).Property("ConsumerId").CurrentValue = null;

If "always explicitly define the foreign key" is the answer, then feel free to close, but I wanted to raise the issue nonetheless, given it's a bit confusing, especially since I could not find any "gotchas" about using a shadow foreign key in the docs. :)

EF Core version: 5.0.7 Database provider: Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL 5.0.7 Target framework: .NET 5.0

item.title: Issue: Add support for basic DateTime properties - @SparkyTD
item.description:
I would like to be able to use some basic date functions like YEAR(), MONTH(), DAY(), HOUR(), etc.. on columns that are mapped to a DateTime property, but this currently seems to be impossible. For example, with basic MySQL, it would look like this:

SELECT * FROM orders GROUP BY DAYOFMONTH(orders.date)

This statement should be the equivalent of the following query:

var query = database.Orders
    .GroupBy(o => o.Date.Day);

But currently EF Core is unable to translate this to the corresponding SQL statement.

I would like to see all the DateTime functions and properties mapped to EF Core databases.

Some more examples:

  • DateTime.Year -> YEAR(date)
  • DateTime.Month -> MONHT(date)
  • DateTime.Day -> DAY(date)
  • DateTime.DayOfWeek -> DAYOFWEEK(date)
  • DateTime.DayOfYear -> DAYOFYEAR(date)
  • DateTime.Hour -> HOUR(date)
  • etc...

item.title: Issue: InMemory and SQL Server provider seems to access TPH shared columns differently - @joakimriedel
item.description:
File a bug

While trying to find a way to access a shared column between multiple classes using TPH (see StackOverflow post here) I learned that there is a difference in how the SqlServer and InMemory providers handle accessing a shared column between multiple classes in TPH.

In SqlServer provider, I can access the shared columns for all overrides using a single cast of one of the types. In InMemory provider, it is more specific so I need to add each override manually (actually more correct imo)

Depending on what is the proper way forward here, this issue is about:

a) Fixing a bug to ensure feature parity between the two providers so that a1) They both need to be specific for each type (like InMemory today) a2) They both will access the shared column when using a single type (like SqlServer today)

or

b) request a feature to access the shared column in a future proof way, perhaps with EF.Property as below (which does not currently work)

What do you suggest from the EF team?

Include your code

class Program
    {
        public static readonly ILoggerFactory MyLoggerFactory
            = LoggerFactory.Create(builder => { 
                builder.AddConsole(); 
            });

        static async Task Main(string[] args)
        {
            using (var context = new ClientContext())
            {
                context.Database.EnsureDeleted();
                context.Database.EnsureCreated();

                var actions = await context.Actions
                    .Select(a => new
                    {
                        Id = a.Id,

                        // this will access Message column in all overrides in SqlServer but not with InMemory
                        Message = (a as ActionB).Message,

                        // this works for both providers - but really messy and complex in real world code
                        //Message = (a as ActionA).Message ?? (a as ActionB).Message,

                        // this throws "Either the query source is not an entity type, or the specified property does not exist on the entity type."
                        // is there any other way to access the shared column Message?
                        // Message = EF.Property<string>(a, "Message"),
                    })
                    .ToListAsync();

                actions.ForEach(a => Console.WriteLine(a.Id + a.Message));
            }
        }

        public class ActionBase
        {
            public int Id { get; set; }

            // ... other shared properties
        }

        public class ActionA : ActionBase
        {
            // shared with B
            [Required]
            [Column("Message")]
            public string Message { get; set; }

            // ... other specific properties
        }

        public class ActionB : ActionBase
        {
            // shared with A
            [Required]
            [Column("Message")]
            public string Message { get; set; }

            // ... other specific properties
        }

        public class ActionC : ActionBase
        {
            public string SomethingElse { get; set; }

            // ... other specific properties
        }

        class ClientContext : DbContext
        {
            protected override void OnConfiguring(DbContextOptionsBuilder optionsBuilder)
            {
                // TO USE SQL
                //optionsBuilder
                //    .UseLoggerFactory(MyLoggerFactory)
                //    .UseSqlServer("Server=(localdb)\\mssqllocaldb;Database=TPHSharedColumn;Trusted_Connection=True;MultipleActiveResultSets=true;Connect Timeout=30")
                //    .EnableSensitiveDataLogging(false);
                // TO USE INMEMORY
                optionsBuilder
                   .UseLoggerFactory(MyLoggerFactory)
                   .UseInMemoryDatabase(Guid.NewGuid().ToString());
            }

            protected override void OnModelCreating(ModelBuilder builder)
            {
                base.OnModelCreating(builder);

                builder.Entity<ActionA>().HasData(new ActionA()
                {
                    Id = 1,
                    Message = "A"
                });
                builder.Entity<ActionB>().HasData(new ActionB()
                {
                    Id = 2,
                    Message = "B"
                });
                builder.Entity<ActionC>().HasData(new ActionC()
                {
                    Id = 3,
                    SomethingElse = "C"
                });
            }

            public DbSet<ActionBase> Actions { get; set; }
        }
    }

Expected output

Running the above code with any valid access method, I expect the output to be

1A
2B
3

for both providers.

Include provider and version information

EF Core version: 5.0.7 or 6.0.0-preview.5.21301.9 Database provider: SqlServer and InMemory Target framework: .NET 5.0

item.title: Issue: Not able to use Computed/Generated columns. - @chravikishore
item.description:
Not able to use Computed/Generated columns.

I am using the HasComputedColumnSql extension like below

entityTypeBuilder
               .Property(e => e.ConversionRate)
               .HasComputedColumnSql("(BaseUnitQuantity * 1.0)/Quantity")
               .IsRequired();

and the migration generated the script successfully as below

migrationBuilder.CreateTable(
                name: "MyTable",
                columns: table => new
                {
                    ...............
                    ConversionRate = table.Column<decimal>(nullable: false, computedColumnSql: "(BaseUnitQuantity * 1.0)/Quantity")
                },

But when I update the database it is giving following error

Failed executing DbCommand (0ms) [Parameters=[], CommandType='Text', CommandTimeout='30']
CREATE TABLE "MyTable" (
    "ColID" INTEGER NOT NULL CONSTRAINT "PK_MyTable" PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    "Col1" INTEGER NOT NULL,
    "Quantity" INTEGER NOT NULL,
    "Col2" INTEGER NOT NULL,
    "BaseUnitQuantity" INTEGER NOT NULL,
    "ConversionRate" AS (CAST(BaseUnitQuantity AS DECIMAL)/Quantity),
...........
);
Disposing transaction.
Closing connection to database 'main' on server '.......\bin\Debug\netcoreapp3.1\.....db'.
Closed connection to database 'main' on server '......\bin\Debug\netcoreapp3.1\.....db'.
'....DataContext' disposed.
Microsoft.Data.Sqlite.SqliteException (0x80004005): SQLite Error 1: 'near "AS": syntax error'.
   at Microsoft.Data.Sqlite.SqliteException.ThrowExceptionForRC(Int32 rc, sqlite3 db)
   at Microsoft.Data.Sqlite.SqliteCommand.PrepareAndEnumerateStatements(Stopwatch timer)+MoveNext()
   at Microsoft.Data.Sqlite.SqliteCommand.GetStatements(Stopwatch timer)+MoveNext()
   at Microsoft.Data.Sqlite.SqliteDataReader.NextResult()
   at Microsoft.Data.Sqlite.SqliteCommand.ExecuteReader(CommandBehavior behavior)
   at Microsoft.Data.Sqlite.SqliteCommand.ExecuteNonQuery()
   at Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Storage.RelationalCommand.ExecuteNonQuery(RelationalCommandParameterObject parameterObject)
   at Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Migrations.MigrationCommand.ExecuteNonQuery(IRelationalConnection connection, IReadOnlyDictionary`2 parameterValues)
   at Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Migrations.Internal.MigrationCommandExecutor.ExecuteNonQuery(IEnumerable`1 migrationCommands, IRelationalConnection connection)
   at Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Migrations.Internal.Migrator.Migrate(String targetMigration)
   at Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design.Internal.MigrationsOperations.UpdateDatabase(String targetMigration, String connectionString, String contextType)
   at Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design.OperationExecutor.UpdateDatabaseImpl(String targetMigration, String connectionString, String contextType)
   at Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design.OperationExecutor.UpdateDatabase.<>c__DisplayClass0_0.<.ctor>b__0()
   at Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design.OperationExecutor.OperationBase.Execute(Action action)
SQLite Error 1: 'near "AS": syntax error'.

EF Core version: 5.0.7 Database provider: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Sqlite Target framework: netcoreapp3.1 Operating system: Windows IDE: Visual Studio 2019 16.3
